title: Photon Number Coherence of a Quantum Dot-Cavity System Excited Using the SUPER Scheme
authors: Paul C. A. Hagen, Thomas Bracht, Mathieu Bozzio, Moritz Cygorek, Doris E. Reiter, Philip Walther, Vollrath M. Axt
abstract: To fulfill the security requirements of quantum cryptography, photon number coherence (PNC) of single photon sources has recently become an important figure of merit. Quantum dots (QDs) embedded in photonic microcavities offer a mature source of single photons, of which many properties can be tuned by the use of different excitation protocols or parameters. We show that the Swing-UP of quantum EmitteR population (SUPER) scheme can significantly decrease the PNC of the emitted photon, compared to resonant excitation. The reason for this is a laser-induced Stark shift, which effectively decouples the QD from the cavity during the SUPER excitation. Our calculations account for environmental effects such as phonons and radiative losses.
summary: The paper shows that the SUPER excitation scheme can reduce photon number coherence in quantum dot-cavity systems by using a laser-induced Stark shift to decouple the dot from the cavity during excitation. This reduction is beneficial for certain quantum cryptographic protocols like BB84. The study also quantifies how phonon-induced effects contribute to the loss of coherence.
main_problem: Investigating how the SUPER (Swing-UP of quantum EmitteR population) excitation scheme affects the photon number coherence (PNC) of a quantum dot-cavity system, which is a critical metric for quantum cryptography security.
main_result: The SUPER scheme significantly decreases the PNC compared to resonant excitation because a laser-induced Stark shift effectively decouples the quantum dot from the cavity during the excitation pulse.
method: Numerical solution of the Lindblad-form von Neumann equation using the process tensor matrix product operators implemented in the ACE code, accounting for phonon-induced dephasing.
model_or_system: A two-level quantum dot embedded in a single-mode photonic microcavity, coupled to a longitudinal acoustic (LA) phonon environment and subject to radiative/cavity losses.
key_observables: Photon number coherence (PNC), QD electronic occupation, electronic coherence, and cavity Fock state occupation.
important_parameters: Pulse duration (2 ps), temperature (4.2 K), coupling strength (g = 0.05 meV), and laser-induced Stark shift.
main_assumptions: The system is modeled with up to two photons in the cavity and the phonon environment is treated via a pure-dephasing Hamiltonian.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the level scheme and physical processes; Fig 2 compares QD occupation, electronic coherence, and cavity dynamics between RE and SUPER schemes; Fig 3 shows integrated QD/cavity dynamics; Fig 4 demonstrates the impact of phonons on the decay of occupation and coherence.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of PNC in quantum cryptography, describes the physical model and the SUPER excitation scheme, presents numerical results comparing SUPER to resonant excitation, analyzes the role of the Stark shift and phonons, and concludes with the implications for quantum key distribution.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for researchers in quantum optics and open quantum systems as it demonstrates how specific laser-driven protocols can be used to manipulate photonic coherence and decouple light-matter interaction via the Stark shift.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Level scheme of the QD-cavity system that is excited using the SUPER scheme and couples to longitudinal acoustic phonons. The lasers Ω1/2(t) are both red-detuned from the exciton-transition by ℏω1/2 X. The QD couples to the cavity through the coupling strength g and can decay with a rate γ, while the cavity decay rate is κ. The exciton also couples to the environment of longitudinal acoustic (LA) phonons.

FIG. 2. a) Shows the QD occupation for resonant excitation (red) and SUPER (blue) in dependence on time. b) shows the respective absolute electronic coherences. The cavity occupations and coherences between 0 and 1 photons in the cavity are shown in c), together with schematics illustrating the physical processes occurring for RE and SUPER, respectively. Both SUPER pulses and the RE pulse have identical durations with σ = 2 ps. The RE pulse area was chosen by minimizing ˜ρ01 −˜ρ11 to make sure low coherence and high occupation is achieved. All calculations were done at 4.2 K.

FIG. 3. Integrated values of a) the occupations and b) the coherences for the QD (orange) and the cavity photons (green) in dependence on the second SUPER pulse area, while the first pulse area is kept constant. c) Shows the PNC, which is obtained for resonant excitation with a pulse of equal duration σ = 2 ps to the SUPER pulses in a) and b).

FIG. 4. The electronic occupation ρXX and the absolute value of the coherence |ρGx| at time 10 ps after the pulse maximum. The solid (dashed) lines show the results with (without) phonons. Fig. 2 uses Θ2 = 15.78 π.
